data.text/markdown:
Singular gradients, conformal flows, and Fourier shrinkage

Akhil Nair · April 2026

data.text/html: <marimo-callout-output data-html='"<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\">The paper [Sahraee-Ardakan, Delbracio &amp; Milanfar, 2026] proves that noise-blind diffusion samplers implicitly run a Riemannian gradient flow on a singular energy landscape, and that a posterior-averaged conformal factor cancels the singularity. The figure below shows the cancellation directly on a 4-point dataset where the math has a closed form. The notebook adds an original extension: an exact Fourier-mode picture of the same flow on Gaussian random fields, with a closed-form half-power cutoff <marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||(k_c(t, n_s) = (a(t)/b(t))^{2/n_s}||)</marimo-tex>.</span></span>"' data-kind='"neutral"'></marimo-callout-output>

data.text/markdown: Lead figure. Five fixed probes plus a live red probe, evaluated at every noise level ||(t||) on a 4-corner discrete dataset. Panel (a) is the per-||(t||) integrand of ||(\nabla E_{\text{marg}}||), which tracks the dashed ||(1/b(t)^2||) envelope on off-data probes. Panel (c) is the product with ||(\lambda(t)^2||) from panel (b), bounded across the divergent regime. The cancellation is the closed-form mechanism behind the paper's stability theorem.

data.text/html: Interactive controls (live on cloud / wasm). Click the plane to pick a probe; the red curve in the figure above recomputes in closed form on every click.

data.text/markdown:
Contents

  1. The marginal energy and its preconditioner
  2. Why the raw gradient is singular
  3. Parameterization stability
  4. OLS scaling check 4b. Tiny MLP recovers the closed form
  5. Extension: Fourier-mode shrinkage
  6. Limits
  7. References

data.text/html: 1. The marginal energy and its preconditioner

On smooth Gaussian data the marginal energy has no Dirac atoms, so neither field above is singular. The conformal preconditioner still reshapes the flow: ||(\bar\lambda(u)||) contracts the field more where the posterior ||(p(t \mid u)||) concentrates on small ||(t||). Hover for exact ||((u_1, u_2, \text{value})||) readouts.
||[\nabla_u \log p(u \mid t) = \frac{a(t) D_t^*(u) - u}{b(t)^2}\qquad \text{(Tweedie / Robbins, paper Eq. 10)}||]||[\nabla_u E_{\text{marg}}(u) = \mathbb{E}_{t \mid u}\!\left[\frac{u - a(t)\,D_t^*(u)}{b(t)^2}\right]\qquad \text{(paper Eq. 11)}||]
↑ Top
2. Why the raw gradient is singular

<marimo-ui-element object-id='qnkX-0' random-id='acd0ee58-db8a-7b9c-7c91-c4c9e5dbb075'><marimo-tabs data-initial-value='""' data-label='null' data-tabs='["<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\">4 discrete corners</span></span>","<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\">1D circle in 2D</span></span>"]'><div data-kind='tab'><marimo-mime-renderer data-mime='"application/vnd.marimo+mimebundle"' data-data='"{\"image/png\": \"data:image/png;base64,\", \"__metadata__\": {\"image/png\": {\"width\": 939, \"height\": 430}}}"'></marimo-mime-renderer></div><div data-kind='tab'><marimo-mime-renderer data-mime='"application/vnd.marimo+mimebundle"' data-data='"{\"image/png\": \"data:image/png;base64,\", \"__metadata__\": {\"image/png\": {\"width\": 939, \"height\": 430}}}"'></marimo-mime-renderer></div></marimo-tabs></marimo-ui-element>

On-data probes (at corners or on the symmetry axis) shrink to zero. Off-data probes plateau at a probe-dependent constant. The shaded band marks ||(t \in [0.01, 0.5]||), where the raw gradient diverges as ||(1/b^2||); slopes in the legend are log-log fits over that band.
For a probe ||(u||) near a datum ||(x_k||) and small ||(t||): ||[u - a(t)\,D_t^*(u) \;\approx\; \|u - x_k\|\qquad (a \to 1,\; D_t^* \to x_k)||]||[\sigma^2(t) \;\approx\; b(t)^2 + a(t)^2 \cdot \mathrm{jitter} \;\to\; b(t)^2\qquad (\mathrm{jitter} \ll b^2)||]||[\Rightarrow \quad \Bigl\| \frac{u - a D_t^*}{\sigma^2} \Bigr\| \;\approx\; \frac{\|u - x_k\|}{b(t)^2} \quad \square||]
↑ Top
3. Parameterization stability

<marimo-callout-output data-html='"<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\"><strong>Symbolic verification (sympy).</strong> Velocity, <marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||((c, d) = (-1, 1)||)</marimo-tex> under FM linear: <marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||(\\nu(t) = 1||)</marimo-tex> (paper Eq. 70).  Noise, <marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||((c, d) = (0, 1)||)</marimo-tex>: <marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||(\\nu(t) = - \\frac{1}{t - 1}||)</marimo-tex>, envelope <marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||(\\dot b / b = \\frac{1}{t}||)</marimo-tex>, and <marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||(\\lim_{t \\to 0^+} \\nu / (\\dot b/b) = 0||)</marimo-tex>. The literal noise <marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||(\\nu||)</marimo-tex> is bounded near the manifold under FM linear; the divergence the paper attributes to noise prediction lives in <marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||(\\dot b / b||)</marimo-tex>, not in <marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||(\\nu||)</marimo-tex> itself.</span></span>"' data-kind='"info"'></marimo-callout-output>

data.text/markdown: ↑ Top

data.text/html: 4. OLS scaling: linear regression recovers the closed form

data.text/markdown: ↑ Top

data.text/html: 4b. A tiny MLP recovers the same closed form

Drift-weighted error multiplies the per-sample residual by the sampler gain ||(|\nu(t)|||) that the integrator sees. Velocity gain is identically 1 (paper Eq. 70), so its curve stays flat as the training window approaches ||(t = 0||). Noise gain is ||(|\dot b / b| = 1/t||) under FM linear, so its curve climbs by an order of magnitude as ||(t_{\min}||) shrinks from 0.5 to 0.005.This is the paper's stability theorem visible on a real trained model: the divergence lives in the integrator weight, not in raw training loss.↑ Top
5. Extension: Fourier-mode shrinkage on Gaussian random fields

class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\">$<marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||(k_c(t, n_s) \\;=\\; \\bigl(a(t)/b(t)\\bigr)^{2/n_s}||)</marimo-tex>$  Setting the per-mode signal-fraction <marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||(W = 1/2||)</marimo-tex> in <marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||(a^2 \\sigma_k^2 = b^2||)</marimo-tex> with <marimo-tex class=\"arithmatex\">||(\\sigma_k^2 = k^{-n_s}||)</marimo-tex> gives the closed form.</span></span>"' data-kind='"info"'></marimo-callout-output></div></div></marimo-tabs></marimo-ui-element>

data.text/markdown: Each tab is self-contained. The shrinkage tab carries its own ||(t||) slider and ||(n_s||) dropdown bound to the heatmap; the forward/reverse-flow tab carries a scrub-and-play widget plus a static 2x4 strip fallback in a collapsed accordion. The ||(k_c||) closed form is what the paper does not state explicitly: it isolates the moving boundary between signal-preserved and noise-dominated modes as a function of ||((t, n_s)||).
↑ Top

6. Limits

↑ Top

data.text/markdown:
7. References

  • [1] Sahraee-Ardakan, M., Delbracio, M., Milanfar, P. The Geometry of Noise: Why Diffusion Models Don't Need Noise Conditioning. arXiv:2602.18428v1, Google, February 2026.
  • [2] Sun, Q., Jiang, Z., Zhao, H., He, K. Is noise conditioning necessary for denoising generative models? arXiv:2502.13129, 2025.
  • [3] Lipman, Y., Chen, R. T. Q., Ben-Hamu, H., Nickel, M., Le, M. Flow Matching for Generative Modeling. arXiv:2210.02747, 2023.
  • [4] Wang, R., Du, Y. Equilibrium Matching: Generative Modeling with Implicit Energy-Based Models. arXiv:2510.02300, 2025.
  • [5] Salimans, T., Ho, J. Progressive Distillation for Fast Sampling of Diffusion Models. ICLR 2022.
Source: geometry-of-noise-molab/. ↑ Top
